items.q: What does a liquidity ratio like the current ratio measure?
items.correct: A business's ability to meet short-term obligations — current assets divided by current liabilities. A ratio above 1 means assets cover liabilities; too high may indicate poor use of resources.
items.misconception: A higher liquidity ratio is always better.
items.random_wrong: Liquidity ratios measure how quickly a business sells products.
items.why: Very high ratios can signal idle cash or excessive stock — capital that could earn returns. Liquidity is about meeting bills on time, not maximising assets. Students assume bigger is better and miss the trade-off.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: Why are hygiene factors and motivators not on the same scale in Herzberg's theory?
items.correct: Hygiene factors only prevent dissatisfaction (their absence demotivates, presence is neutral); motivators create satisfaction (their presence motivates, absence is neutral). They affect different outcomes, not different points on one scale.
items.misconception: Hygiene factors and motivators are just two types of motivation that combine.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness only.
items.why: Herzberg's key insight is dual-factor: satisfaction and dissatisfaction are not opposites but separate dimensions. Students collapse them to a single motivation scale, missing the structural claim.

items.q: Schumpeter described entrepreneurs as agents of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Creative destruction. “Marginal utility” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of UK start-ups fail within three years?
items.why: It's 60%. “20%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Dyson launch his bagless cyclone vacuum?
items.why: The real figure is 1993 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
